retrieved_docs.title: Pinecone Blog — Knowledge Infrastructure For Agents
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Pinecone Blog — Knowledge Infrastructure For Agents

The Primary User Is Changing
Every technological paradigm shift produces a defining data infrastructure category. Relational databases for client-server. Object stores for clouds. Vector databases for Assistive AI.
Pinecone built the market-leading vector database. 800,000+ active developers and 9,000+ paying customers run their AI on it: semantic search, recommendation systems, retrieval-augmented generation. We defined the category.
That Assistive AI category was built for a human user; type a query, get relevant documents back. It worked.
Now agents are surpassing humans as the primary consumers of knowledge infrastructure. In this Agentic AI era, agents are performing tasks, stuck in brute-force loops. Retrieve a set of chunks. Read them. Realize something is missing. Retrieve more. Synthesize. Hit a conflict. Retrieve again. Roughly 85% of an agent’s effort is spent on knowledge retrieval and the output still requires human review before anyone can act on it.
The inevitable result: task completion rates stuck at 50–60%. Unpredictable latency that kills production SLOs. Outputs no enterprise can govern. And, downstream of all of it, runaway token costs. This is the “ten blue li

retrieved_docs.title: Tavily — about
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Tavily — about

/about us
The agentic web is vast and unstructured. Tavily delivers high-quality, fresh, and structured web context, making it easy for agents to stay grounded as they scale from prototypes to production.
/our mission
Our mission is to help AI agents navigate the web quickly and efficiently by shaping the context they see. We prioritize relevance, freshness, and efficiency so agents reason accurately without wasting tokens.
/team
We’re a team of collaborators, builders, and deep thinkers, obsessed with making reliable web access possible for AI agents in the real world. We move quickly, support each other, and stay focused on shipping systems that deliver real impact in production.

retrieved_docs.title: Qdrant Blog — Qdrant Skills Release
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Qdrant Blog — Qdrant Skills Release

Qdrant Skills for AI Agents
Thierry Damiba
·March 31, 2026
On this page:
The standard RAG tutorial teaches a simple pattern: embed your documents, store them in a vector database, retrieve the top K, and feed them to the LLM. The vector engine is passive infrastructure. Put vectors in, get neighbors out. Configure once, forget about it forever.
This mental model is why most AI agents treat vector search as a black box. They can call the API. They cannot make the engineering decisions that determine whether it works well.
We built Qdrant skills to change that: deep Qdrant knowledge, encoded so agents (and humans if you’re reading this!) can navigate it.
Vector search has evolved
Production vector search is built from independent primitives that interact, not a single retrieval call:
- Quantization for memory compression
- HNSW parameter tuning for recall and latency tradeoffs
- Payload filtering with indexed fields
- Fusion strategies for hybrid retrieval (dense + sparse)
- Sharding and replication for scale
Each one of these is a lever. Pull one and it changes the others. Enable binary quantization and you cut memory 32x, but you need oversampling with rescore to preserve recall. M

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025

The Latent Space newsletter surveys the agentic AI platform landscape as of mid-2025. The analysis identifies three dominant approaches: (1) orchestration frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph) focused on multi-agent coordination; (2) RAG-first platforms (LlamaIndex, Vertex AI Search) focused on grounding agents in enterprise data; and (3) evaluation-native platforms (Langfuse, Braintrust, PromptLayer) focused on reliability and measurement. The newsletter argues that evaluation will become the key differentiator as models commoditize.

retrieved_docs.title: YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure

Y Combinator's Winter 2025 Request for Startups highlights AI infrastructure as a top investment thesis. Key areas include: developer tools for agentic AI (debugging, testing, deployment), evaluation frameworks for LLM reliability, multi-modal data pipelines, and cost-optimization layers for large language model inference. YC notes that startups solving agent observability and grounding are especially well-positioned.

retrieved_docs.title: Exa Blog — anara-exa
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Exa Blog — anara-exa

Anara integrates Exa's Search and Content APIs to power fast, accurate retrieval and summarization of research papers, helping researchers traverse the vast academic landscape while correctly citing works and discovering hidden gems at the frontier.
"Exa's strong coverage and flexible API have been a key differentiator for us. Scientists trust our product further when the relevant papers they expect to see are available to them in the right workflows. There are use cases where we require the full document, others a summary. The ability to customize search based on the use case easily, is important."— Naveed Janmohamed, Founder & CEO of Anara
About Anara
AI workspace for scientific research
Anara is an AI workspace for scientists, students, and research teams, helping users find and interact with scientific documents using AI. Powered by major academic databases like PubMed, arXiv, and JSTOR, Anara enables collaborative learning by creating searchable libraries for projects and generating tailored quizzes.
Use cases
- Citations - Identify and retrieve original sources to drive accurate citation in writing
- Workflow agents - Power agents that search papers and suggest next steps in

retrieved_docs.title: Exa — about
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Exa — about

Dear curious internet wanderer,
Welcome to Exa. Exa is an applied AI lab building a search engine unlike the world has ever seen.
We don't have ads. We sell our search as an API, optimized exclusively for quality, latency, and customizability. That's why customers like Cursor, Lovable, and thousands of others use us to power agents and workflows.
But that's just today. Exa's ultimate goal is perfect search.
What is perfect search? It's simple – you should be able to know anything about the world. Literally anything, like:
Perfect search is highly controllable, unbiased, comprehensive. Because we lack it, we each walk around with an incomplete understanding of nearly everything.
Building perfect search will be extremely hard. But it's also necessary. It's critical civilizational infrastructure for our new AI reality.
Politics is fragmenting, wars are raging, and technology is accelerating -- we need tools that deeply inform us what's going on. If we don't gain control over the planet's information, we will lose control over our planet.
In short, the world needs perfect search. It needs an organization with pure incentives to build it. And because no one else is doing it, that's why

retrieved_docs.title: Google updates AI search to include quotes from Reddit and other sources
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Google updates AI search to include quotes from Reddit and other sources

Google is updating search to refine its AI experience by adding additional context to links, like excerpts from web forums and blogs, as well as a feature that highlights links from a user’s news subscriptions.
While citing web forums and discussion boards can help users find answers to more niche queries, this design choice could also prove chaotic.
Two years ago, Google overhauled its search experience to put AI front and center — when you search for something, Google will often summon an “AI Overview,” which has spurred mixed reception from users. People quickly pointed out how the feature could be exploited, since it failed to recognize sarcasm or information that comes from dubious sources. (It cited The Onion when telling someone to eat “one small rock per day,” and used Reddit to advise someone to put glue on their pizza to make the cheese stick better.)
Though Google’s AI Overviews have improved significantly, they still — like anything powered by an LLM — are prone to hallucination. A recent New York Times analysis found that the AI Overviews were correct about nine times out of 10. But for a company that processes trillions of queries a year, that success rate would mean
